question:
Let's talk about shopping.

Question 1: Tell me about your favorite place to shop. What kind of store is it, and what makes it special compared to other places? Describe the atmosphere, the products they carry, and why you keep going back there.

Question 2: Think about a time when you had a particularly memorable shopping experience — either really good or really bad. What happened from beginning to end? How did you handle the situation, and what was the outcome?

Question 3: Shopping habits have changed dramatically over the years, especially with the rise of online shopping and new technologies. How have your own shopping habits evolved over time? Compare how you used to shop in the past with how you shop now, and how do you think shopping will change in the future?
key_expressions: curated selection, element of surprise, flash sale, conscious consumer, game-changer
tip: 콤보 세트는 3개 질문이 하나의 주제로 연결되므로, 일관된 스토리라인을 유지하는 것이 핵심입니다. Q1(묘사)→Q2(과거 경험)→Q3(비교/변화) 순서로 난이도가 올라가므로, Q3에서 과거-현재-미래를 시간 순서대로 구조화하여 답변하세요. AL 등급을 위해서는 단순 나열이 아니라 구체적 에피소드, 감정 표현, 원인-결과 분석을 포함해야 하며, 'It was more of a social activity than anything else'처럼 자신의 행동을 메타적으로 분석하는 표현을 활용하면 높은 점수를 받을 수 있습니다. 또한 각 답변을 60초 이상 유지하되, 핵심 포인트 2~3개를 중심으로 살을 붙이는 전략이 효과적입니다.
sample_answer:
Question 1:
So, my favorite place to shop would have to be this huge outlet mall called Yeoju Premium Outlets. It's about an hour's drive from Seoul, but honestly, it's totally worth the trip. What makes it special is that they carry all the major brands — like Nike, Adidas, Coach, and even high-end ones like Gucci and Prada — but at significantly discounted prices, sometimes up to 70 percent off. The atmosphere is really pleasant because it's an open-air mall, so you're basically walking around outside, which feels way more relaxing than being stuck in a cramped indoor shopping center. They also have a great food court with everything from Korean BBQ to Italian pasta, so I usually make a whole day out of it. The reason I keep going back is that I always end up finding great deals there. Every time I visit, there's something new on sale, and I just can't resist a good bargain. Plus, the staff are generally really friendly and helpful, which makes the whole experience enjoyable.

Question 2:
Oh, I have a story for this one. A couple of years ago, I ordered a laptop online from a well-known electronics retailer. It was during Black Friday, so I managed to snag a really good deal — like 40 percent off the original price. I was so excited. But when the package arrived, I opened it up and realized they had sent me a completely different model. It was a cheaper one with lower specs, and I was honestly pretty frustrated. So I immediately called their customer service, and at first, the representative kept insisting that I had ordered the wrong item, which was ridiculous because I had the confirmation email right in front of me. I stayed calm but firm, and I asked to speak with a supervisor. The supervisor was much more understanding and apologized for the mix-up. They arranged for a return pickup the very next day and sent me the correct laptop with express shipping at no extra cost. On top of that, they gave me a 10 percent discount coupon for my next purchase as compensation. So it turned out to be a pretty positive experience in the end, but it definitely taught me to always double-check my order details and keep all the receipts and confirmation emails.

Question 3:
Wow, shopping has really transformed over the years, hasn't it? When I was younger, like in my teens and early twenties, I used to go to physical stores for pretty much everything. I'd spend entire weekends at department stores or shopping malls with my friends, trying on clothes, testing out products in person, and just browsing around. Back then, the only way to compare prices was to literally walk from store to store, which was exhausting but also kind of fun in its own way.

But now, I'd say about 80 percent of my shopping is done online. I use apps like Coupang, which is basically Korea's version of Amazon, and I love how convenient it is. I can compare prices across multiple platforms in seconds, read customer reviews, and have everything delivered to my doorstep — sometimes even the same day. I also use a lot of subscription services now for things like groceries and household essentials, which saves me so much time.

As for the future, I think shopping will become even more personalized through AI and augmented reality. Imagine being able to virtually try on clothes using your phone or having an AI assistant that knows your style preferences and automatically suggests items you'd love. I also think drone delivery will become mainstream, making same-day delivery the standard rather than the exception. It's kind of exciting to think about, honestly.

question: I'd like to give you a situation and ask you to act it out. You are planning to have a picnic at a local park this weekend with your friends. Call the park's management office and ask three to four questions about the park's facilities, rules, and any events happening this weekend.
key_expressions: I was wondering whether, I'd like to ask about your policy regarding, on a first-come first-served basis, Are there any restrictions on, Could you confirm whether that's the case
tip: 롤플레이 문제에서는 반드시 상황에 완전히 몰입하여 실제 전화하는 것처럼 자연스러운 톤을 유지하세요. AL 등급을 위해 단순 질문 나열이 아닌, 각 질문에 배경 설명(왜 묻는지)을 덧붙이고, 간접 의문문(I was wondering if ~, Could you tell me ~)을 활용하여 격식 있는 표현력을 보여주세요. 마무리 인사까지 빠짐없이 하는 것이 중요합니다.
sample_answer:
Hi there, good morning! I'm calling because my friends and I are planning to have a picnic at your park this coming weekend, and I just wanted to ask a few questions before we finalize our plans, if you don't mind.

First of all, I was wondering if the park has any designated picnic areas with tables and benches that we could use. There are going to be about eight of us, so it would be really nice if we could reserve a spot, especially one with a shade structure or near some trees, because honestly, the weather has been pretty brutal lately. Is there any way we can make a reservation in advance, or is it strictly first-come, first-served?

Secondly, I wanted to ask about the barbecue situation. Are there any public grills available, or would we need to bring our own portable one? And if we do bring our own, are there any specific rules we should be aware of? Like, are charcoal grills allowed, or do you only permit gas grills? My buddy has a small charcoal grill he swears by, but I just want to make sure we're not breaking any regulations.

Also, I'm curious — are there any special events or festivals happening at the park this weekend? The reason I ask is that last time we went to a different park for a get-together, there happened to be a huge music festival going on, and it was absolutely packed. We could barely find a parking spot, let alone a quiet area to sit down and enjoy ourselves. So I just want to avoid that kind of situation if possible.

Oh, and one more thing — what are the park hours this weekend? I know some parks have extended hours during the spring and summer, and we were thinking of staying until the evening to watch the sunset. Is the park open until dark, or is there a specific closing time we need to keep in mind?

I really appreciate your help with all of this. We're super excited about the picnic, and I just want to make sure everything goes smoothly. Thanks so much!

question: Tell me about the place where you currently live. What does your home look like? Describe your neighborhood and what makes it a special place for you. I'd like to hear everything from the layout of your home to the overall atmosphere of your area.
key_expressions: open-concept layout, breathtaking panoramic view, within walking distance, hustle and bustle, sense of community
tip: AL 등급을 위해서는 단순히 집 묘사에 그치지 말고, 왜 그 집이 특별한지 개인적인 감정과 경험을 엮어서 답변하세요. 공간 묘사(시각적 디테일) → 주변 환경의 장점 → 개인적 애착의 흐름으로 답변을 구조화하면 자연스럽고 풍부한 답변이 됩니다. 또한 'what I appreciate most', 'one thing that truly sets it apart' 같은 전환 표현을 활용하면 답변의 논리적 흐름이 크게 향상됩니다.
sample_answer:
Sure, I'd love to tell you about where I live. I currently live in a mid-sized apartment in Gangnam, Seoul, and honestly, I couldn't be happier with the location. It's a pretty modern building, probably built around ten years ago, and my unit is on the 12th floor, so I get a really nice view of the city skyline, especially at night when all the lights come on.

In terms of the layout, it's a two-bedroom apartment with an open-plan living room and kitchen. The moment you walk in, there's a small hallway with a shoe rack, and then it opens up into the living area. I've set it up with a big L-shaped couch and a wall-mounted TV, which is basically where I spend most of my downtime binge-watching shows or just chilling after work. The kitchen is nothing fancy, but it's got enough counter space for me to cook simple meals, which I try to do as often as I can to save money.

What really makes my neighborhood special, though, is the convenience. There's literally everything within walking distance — convenience stores, cafes, a gym, and even a small park right behind my building. I usually take a walk there in the evenings when the weather's nice, and it's kind of my way of decompressing after a long day. There's also a subway station just a five-minute walk away, so commuting is a breeze.

Another thing I really appreciate is the vibe of the neighborhood. It's not too quiet and not too hectic — it strikes this perfect balance where you feel like you're in the middle of everything, but you can still get some peace and quiet when you need it. My neighbors are pretty chill too. We're not super close or anything, but we always say hi in the elevator, and there's this unspoken mutual respect about noise levels, which I think is really important when you're living in an apartment complex.

So yeah, all in all, I feel really fortunate to be living where I am. It checks all the boxes for me — convenience, comfort, and a decent atmosphere. I don't see myself moving anytime soon, to be honest.

question: Tell me about your typical routine when you travel abroad. What do you usually do from the moment you arrive at the airport to the time you settle into your hotel? Walk me through your usual process step by step.
key_expressions: make it a point to, have a well-established routine, learn from experience, combat jet lag, hidden gems that guidebooks miss
tip: 습관/루틴 문제는 시간 순서대로 자연스럽게 흐름을 이어가는 것이 핵심입니다. 'First, Then, After that, Once, Finally' 같은 시퀀스 마커를 적극 활용하고, 단순 나열이 아니라 각 단계마다 '왜 그렇게 하는지(이유)'와 '과거 경험에서 배운 점'을 곁들이면 AL 등급다운 깊이 있는 답변이 됩니다. 또한 'I always~', 'I tend to~', 'I make it a habit to~' 같은 습관 표현을 반복적으로 사용하여 루틴임을 명확히 드러내세요.
sample_answer:
Oh, I actually have a pretty set routine whenever I travel abroad, so let me walk you through it. First of all, I always try to get to the airport at least two hours before my flight, sometimes even three for international flights. As soon as I arrive, I head straight to the check-in counter to drop off my luggage. I usually check in online the night before, but I still need to tag my bags and get them on the belt.

Once that's done, I go through security and immigration. Honestly, this is the part I dread the most because the lines can be ridiculously long, especially during peak season. After I get through, I like to grab a coffee at one of the airport cafés and just browse around the duty-free shops. I'm not a big shopper, but I always end up picking up some snacks or maybe a small bottle of perfume.

When I land at my destination, the first thing I do is turn on my phone and connect to Wi-Fi. I usually have my hotel directions saved on Google Maps beforehand, so I don't have to scramble. Then I go through immigration again, pick up my luggage at the baggage claim, and exchange some currency if I haven't already done it through my bank app.

For getting to the hotel, I typically book an airport shuttle or use a ride-hailing app like Uber or Grab, depending on the country. I try to avoid taxis at the airport because, you know, they tend to overcharge tourists. Once I arrive at the hotel, I check in, throw my bags in the room, and the very first thing I do is take a long shower. Traveling makes me feel so grimy, honestly. After freshening up, I usually take a quick nap if it's been a long flight, or if I still have energy, I'll head out to explore the area around the hotel, maybe find a local restaurant for dinner.

One thing I always make sure to do is ask the front desk for a map or some recommendations for nearby attractions. I find that hotel staff usually know the best hidden gems that you won't find on travel blogs. So yeah, that's pretty much my routine — it's nothing fancy, but it works for me every single time.

question: I'd like to talk about weather and seasons. Have you ever been in a situation where extreme weather completely ruined your plans or put you in an unexpected predicament? Tell me what happened, how you dealt with it, and whether that experience changed the way you prepare for weather-related situations now.
key_expressions: as fate would have it, out of nowhere, nerve-wracking experience, a real wake-up call, better to be over-prepared than caught off guard
tip: 돌발 질문은 예상치 못한 주제가 나오므로 당황하지 않는 것이 핵심입니다. '극단적 날씨로 계획이 망가진 경험'이라는 구체적 에피소드를 하나 준비해두세요. 답변 구조는 ①상황 설정(언제/어디서/무엇을) → ②문제 발생(구체적 묘사) → ③대처 방법 → ④교훈/변화 순으로 전개하면 논리적이고 풍부한 답변이 됩니다. AL 등급답게 감정 표현(harrowing, nerve-wracking)과 고급 연결어(as fate would have it, naturally)를 적극 활용하고, 과거와 현재를 대비시켜 성장 서사를 보여주세요.
sample_answer:
Oh, absolutely. There was this one time a couple of years ago that I will never forget. My friends and I had planned a weekend hiking trip to Seoraksan, and we had been looking forward to it for weeks. We checked the forecast beforehand, and it was supposed to be clear skies the entire weekend. So we packed light — no rain gear, no extra layers, nothing.

Well, as soon as we hit the trail on Saturday morning, the sky just turned completely dark out of nowhere. Within an hour, we got caught in this torrential downpour with crazy strong winds. I'm talking sideways rain, the kind that soaks you to the bone in seconds. The trail became super slippery, and we could barely see where we were going. One of my friends actually slipped and twisted her ankle, which made things even worse. We had no choice but to abandon the hike and slowly make our way back down.

By the time we reached the parking lot, we were drenched, freezing, and honestly pretty miserable. We ended up just sitting in the car with the heater blasting, eating convenience store kimbap instead of enjoying the amazing mountaintop view we had been dreaming about. It was such a letdown.

But honestly, that experience was a real wake-up call for me. Ever since then, I've completely changed how I prepare for any outdoor activity. First of all, I never just check the forecast once anymore — I monitor it obsessively in the days leading up to a trip and look at multiple weather apps because they sometimes give different predictions. I also always pack rain gear and extra layers now, even if the forecast looks perfect. You just never know what Mother Nature is going to throw at you.

On top of that, I started keeping a small emergency kit in my car with things like a first aid kit, a blanket, and some snacks, just in case something unexpected happens again. I guess you could say that one miserable rainy hike actually taught me a valuable lesson about being prepared, so in a weird way, I'm kind of grateful it happened.

question: Tell me about a memorable experience you had while booking or staying at a hotel. Maybe something unexpected happened during the reservation process, or perhaps there was a problem when you arrived. Describe the situation in detail, explain how you handled it, and what you learned from that experience.
key_expressions: the hotel had been overbooked, a blessing in disguise, I tried to remain composed, complimentary upgrade, it turned out to be
tip: 과거 경험 문제에서는 단순 나열이 아니라 '스토리텔링' 구조(배경→문제 발생→대처→결과→교훈)로 답변하세요. AL 등급을 위해서는 감정 묘사(frustrated, exhausted, relieved), 구체적 디테일(장소, 시간, 대화 내용), 그리고 경험에서 얻은 교훈까지 포함해야 합니다. 또한 과거 시제를 정확하게 사용하고, 가정법이나 간접화법 등 고급 문법을 자연스럽게 섞어주면 높은 점수를 받을 수 있습니다.
sample_answer:
Oh, this is a great question. I actually have a pretty memorable hotel experience that I'll never forget. So, about two years ago, I took a trip to Busan with my friends during the summer. We booked a hotel online through a travel app because it looked really nice in the photos and the reviews were pretty decent. The price was reasonable too, so we didn't think twice about it.

But here's the thing — when we actually arrived at the hotel, it was nothing like what we expected. First of all, the lobby was super tiny and kind of run-down. And when we got to our room, it was way smaller than what was shown in the pictures. The view was supposed to be an ocean view, but all we could see was a parking lot. I was honestly so disappointed and frustrated.

So, I went straight to the front desk and politely explained the situation. I showed them the photos from the app and told them this wasn't what we had booked. At first, the staff member seemed a bit defensive, but after I calmly explained everything, they offered to upgrade us to a better room on a higher floor. And luckily, that room was actually amazing — it had a real ocean view and was much more spacious.

What I learned from that experience is that you should always double-check hotel reviews carefully before booking, especially the ones with actual photos from real guests, not just the official ones. Also, I realized that staying calm and being polite when complaining can really make a difference. If I had gotten angry or raised my voice, the staff probably wouldn't have been as willing to help. Since then, whenever I book a hotel, I spend a lot more time reading reviews and comparing different options. I even started using map apps to check the actual location and surroundings before making a reservation. It was a stressful experience at first, but it turned out to be a valuable lesson in the end.

question: Describe the recycling system in your country. What kinds of items do people typically recycle, and how is the recycling process organized in your neighborhood? Please describe in as much detail as possible.
key_expressions: separate waste into multiple categories, becomes second nature, dedicated waste collection area, level of community accountability, boost public participation
tip: 묘사 문제에서는 큰 그림(전체 시스템)에서 시작해 점점 구체적인 디테일(내 동네, 내 아파트)로 좁혀가는 '줌인 전략'이 효과적입니다. 단순 나열이 아니라, 개인적인 경험과 감상(impressive, overwhelming 등 감정 표현)을 섞어주면 AL 등급다운 자연스러운 스토리텔링이 됩니다. 또한 'What I find particularly~', 'I'd say~' 같은 의견 표현을 활용하면 고급 화자의 느낌을 줄 수 있습니다.
sample_answer:
Sure, I'd love to talk about the recycling system in Korea. Honestly, Korea has one of the most well-organized recycling systems I've ever seen, and I'm pretty proud of it, even though it can be a bit of a hassle sometimes.

So, in my neighborhood, we have a very strict waste separation system. We basically have to sort our trash into several categories before throwing it away. The main categories are paper, plastic, glass, cans, and Styrofoam. On top of that, we have to separate food waste from general waste, which is something a lot of foreigners find surprising when they first come to Korea.

Let me walk you through how it works in my apartment complex. We have a designated recycling area on the ground floor of our building, and there are separate bins for each type of recyclable material. Every Wednesday and Saturday, we take our sorted recyclables down to this area. Paper products like newspapers, cardboard boxes, and magazines go into one bin. Plastic bottles and containers go into another, but here's the thing — you have to rinse them out and remove the labels before tossing them in. Glass bottles and metal cans each have their own bins as well.

What I find really unique about Korea is the food waste system. We actually have special biodegradable bags that we have to purchase from the local district office or convenience stores. All food scraps go into these bags, and they get collected separately because the food waste is turned into animal feed or compost. Some newer apartment buildings even have these high-tech food waste machines where you scan your resident card, and it weighs your food waste automatically — so you're basically charged by how much food you throw away. It really makes you think twice about wasting food.

As for general waste, we also have to buy designated trash bags from the government. They come in different sizes, and you pay more for bigger bags. This volume-based system was introduced to encourage people to recycle more and produce less waste, and honestly, I think it works pretty well.

One thing that sometimes frustrates me, though, is that the rules can vary slightly depending on which district you live in. I moved from Gangnam to Mapo a couple of years ago, and I had to relearn some of the sorting rules because they were a little different. But overall, I think Korea's recycling system is really impressive, and it's become such a natural part of daily life that I can't imagine not doing it.

question: I'd like to give you a situation and ask you to act it out. You just noticed that a large unauthorized transaction was made from your bank account while you were traveling abroad. Call your bank's customer service center and explain the situation in detail. Ask them what steps need to be taken to resolve this issue, and express your frustration about how this could have happened despite the security measures they advertise.
key_expressions: unauthorized transaction, fraud detection system, freeze my account immediately, file a dispute / dispute the charge, provisional credit
tip: 돌발 롤플레이 문제에서는 감정 표현(frustration, disappointment)과 구체적인 요구사항을 함께 전달해야 AL 등급을 받을 수 있습니다. 단순히 상황 설명에 그치지 말고, ①문제 상황을 구체적 숫자/장소와 함께 설명 ②감정을 자연스럽게 표현 ③은행 측에 여러 가지 조치를 요구 ④향후 재발 방지책까지 언급하는 4단계 구조로 답변하세요. 또한 'I'm struggling to understand how...', 'This is honestly unacceptable' 같은 고급 불만 표현을 활용하면 어휘 다양성 점수가 올라갑니다.
sample_answer:
Oh my God, hello? Hi, yes, I need to speak with someone about a serious issue with my account right away. My name is Jihoon Park, and my account number is 4821-7703. Look, I'm currently traveling in Vietnam on a business trip, and I just got a notification on my phone that someone made a massive withdrawal of 2,300 dollars from my checking account about thirty minutes ago. I did NOT authorize this transaction at all. I've been in meetings all day, and my card has been sitting in the hotel safe the entire time, so there's no way I made this purchase.

First of all, I need you to freeze my account immediately so no more money can be taken out. Can you do that right now while we're on the phone? And second, I need to know exactly what this transaction was — where it was made, what merchant processed it, and how on earth someone got access to my account information.

Honestly, I am extremely frustrated about this whole situation. Your bank has been running all these commercials about how you have top-notch security systems, fraud detection algorithms, and real-time monitoring. You guys literally advertise that your system catches suspicious activity before it even happens. So can you explain to me how a transaction of over two thousand dollars, made from a completely different country than where I am right now, didn't trigger a single red flag in your system? That's absolutely unacceptable.

I've been a loyal customer with your bank for over seven years, and I've never had any issues until now. But this kind of experience really shakes my trust, you know what I mean? I'm thousands of miles away from home, I'm relying on that account for my travel expenses, and now I'm stuck here without access to my funds.

So here's what I need from you. I need the account frozen, I need a full investigation launched into this unauthorized transaction, and I need a temporary credit issued to my account so I'm not stranded here in a foreign country with no money. Also, I want to know the timeline — how long is this investigation going to take? And if this turns out to be fraud, which I'm a hundred percent sure it is, will I be fully reimbursed? I need clear answers on all of this because right now, I'm seriously considering switching to a different bank if this isn't resolved promptly and professionally.

question: Technology has become an essential part of our daily lives. Describe how you use the internet and technology on a typical day. What devices do you use, what do you do online, and how has your reliance on technology changed over the years?
key_expressions: deeply woven into the fabric of, cloud-based collaboration tools, has become second nature, my reliance has grown exponentially, integrated into virtually every aspect of my life
tip: 묘사 문제에서는 시간 순서(아침→낮→저녁)로 구조화하면 자연스럽습니다. AL 등급을 위해 단순 나열이 아닌 과거와 현재를 비교하며 변화를 분석하고, 개인적인 의견과 구체적인 앱/서비스 이름을 언급하여 답변에 깊이를 더하세요. 'It's worth noting that~', 'Looking back~' 같은 고급 전환 표현을 활용하면 좋습니다.
sample_answer:
Oh, technology? I mean, where do I even begin? It's literally woven into every single aspect of my daily life at this point.

So, let me walk you through a typical day. The moment I wake up, the first thing I do is grab my smartphone. I check my notifications, scroll through the news headlines, and glance at my emails to see if there's anything urgent. I'd say my phone is basically an extension of my hand at this point, which sounds a bit sad, but it's the reality.

Once I get to work, I'm on my laptop pretty much the entire day. I work in a tech-related field, so I'm constantly using the internet for research, collaboration tools like Slack and Google Workspace, and video conferencing platforms like Zoom. I'd say about 90 percent of my work is done online, which would've been unthinkable maybe 15 years ago.

During my commute, I usually stream music on Spotify or listen to podcasts. And when I get home, I tend to unwind by watching Netflix or YouTube on my tablet. I also do a lot of online shopping these days — everything from groceries to clothes. It's just so much more convenient than going to a physical store, especially when I'm exhausted after a long day.

Now, in terms of how my reliance on technology has changed over the years, it's honestly been a dramatic shift. When I was growing up, the internet was something you used occasionally, like to check your email or do a school project. But now, I literally can't imagine a single day without it. I use mobile banking apps instead of going to the bank, I navigate with Google Maps instead of asking for directions, and I even manage my health through fitness tracking apps.

If I'm being totally honest, I sometimes worry that I'm too dependent on technology. Like, if my phone dies or the Wi-Fi goes down, I feel genuinely lost. But at the same time, technology has made my life so much more efficient and connected that I wouldn't want to go back to the way things were. It's kind of a double-edged sword, you know?

question: Tell me about your exercise routine in detail. What kinds of physical activities do you usually do to stay healthy? How often do you work out, and where do you typically exercise? Walk me through what a typical workout session looks like from start to finish.
key_expressions: ingrained part of my daily life, sets the tone for the rest of my day, progressive overload, switch things up to keep it from getting monotonous, a holistic approach
tip: 습관/루틴 문제는 '시간 순서(아침→운동 전→운동 중→운동 후)'로 답변을 구성하면 자연스럽고 체계적입니다. AL 등급을 위해 단순 나열이 아닌, 왜 그렇게 하는지 이유와 개인적 경험(예: 예전에는 ~했지만 지금은 ~한다)을 함께 언급하세요. 또한 운동뿐 아니라 식단, 수면 등 연관 습관까지 확장하면 답변의 깊이가 더해집니다.
sample_answer:
Oh, exercise! That's something I'm pretty passionate about, so let me walk you through my routine. I try to work out at least four to five times a week, and honestly, it's become such a big part of my daily life that I feel kind of off when I skip a day.

So, my go-to place is a gym that's about a ten-minute walk from my apartment. I signed up for a membership a couple of years ago, and it was one of the best decisions I've ever made. On weekdays, I usually hit the gym right after work, around six or seven in the evening. I find that working out after a long day at the office is a great way to blow off steam and clear my head.

Now, let me break down what a typical session looks like. When I first get there, I always start with about ten minutes of stretching and light warm-up exercises, like jumping jacks or jogging on the treadmill at a slow pace. I learned the hard way that skipping the warm-up is a recipe for injury — I actually pulled a muscle in my back once because I jumped straight into heavy lifting without warming up. That was not fun at all.

After the warm-up, I usually split my workouts by muscle group. So, for example, Mondays and Thursdays are for upper body — I do things like bench presses, shoulder presses, and pull-ups. Tuesdays and Fridays are leg days, which honestly, I used to dread, but now I've grown to appreciate them. Squats, lunges, and leg presses are my staples. And on Wednesdays, I focus on cardio and core exercises, like running on the treadmill for about thirty minutes and then doing planks and crunches.

One thing I've gotten really into lately is following workout videos on YouTube. There's this one channel I'm obsessed with that has these high-intensity interval training sessions, and they're absolutely killer. I sometimes do those at home on weekends when I don't feel like going to the gym.

To cool down, I always finish with some light stretching and foam rolling. It makes a huge difference in how sore I feel the next day. And after that, I grab a protein shake on my way out — usually something with banana and peanut butter because, honestly, it tastes amazing.

Overall, I'd say my exercise routine has really transformed not just my physical health but my mental health too. I sleep better, I have more energy throughout the day, and I just feel more confident in general. It took me a while to build this habit, but now I genuinely look forward to it.

question: Tell me about yourself now compared to who you were five or ten years ago. How have you changed as a person? Think about your personality, your values, your priorities, or even your daily habits. What do you think brought about those changes, and how do you feel about the person you've become?
key_expressions: in hindsight, my priorities have shifted dramatically, I've come to realize that, a far cry from who I used to be, it was a turning point for me
tip: 비교 문제에서는 '과거의 나'와 '현재의 나'를 명확하게 대비시키는 구조가 핵심입니다. 단순히 '예전엔 이랬고 지금은 이렇다'로 끝내지 말고, 변화의 계기(trigger)와 그 변화에 대한 본인의 감정·평가까지 덧붙여야 AL 등급다운 깊이가 나옵니다. 시제 전환(과거 → 현재)을 자연스럽게 오가며, 구체적 에피소드를 최소 1~2개 포함하세요. 'used to + 동사원형' vs 'now I + 현재시제' 패턴을 적극 활용하면 대비 효과가 극대화됩니다.
sample_answer:
Oh wow, that's a really interesting question. If I compare myself now to who I was, say, ten years ago, I'd say I'm almost a completely different person. Back then, I was in my early twenties, and honestly, I was pretty reckless and impulsive. I used to make decisions on a whim without really thinking about the consequences. I was also super focused on what other people thought of me — like, I'd go out of my way to fit in, even if it meant compromising my own values.

But over the years, I've gone through a lot of experiences that really shaped who I am today. One major turning point was when I started my first real job. That's when I realized that hard work and consistency matter way more than talent alone. I also went through a rough patch in my late twenties — I lost a close family member, and that experience completely shifted my priorities. I stopped caring so much about superficial things like appearances and started valuing genuine relationships and personal growth.

In terms of daily habits, I've become way more disciplined. I used to stay up until two or three in the morning binge-watching shows and then drag myself out of bed at noon. Now, I wake up early, exercise regularly, and actually plan out my day. I've also picked up reading as a habit, which is something I never would have imagined doing ten years ago.

As for my personality, I think I've become much more patient and empathetic. I used to get frustrated really easily and snap at people over small things. But now, I try to put myself in other people's shoes before reacting. I think that comes from just having more life experience and realizing that everyone is dealing with their own struggles.

Overall, I'm really proud of how far I've come. I'm not perfect by any means, but I genuinely like the person I've become. I feel more grounded, more self-aware, and more at peace with who I am. And I think the biggest lesson I've learned is that change doesn't happen overnight — it's a gradual process that comes from facing challenges and learning from your mistakes.

question: I'd like to give you a situation and ask you to act it out. You are planning a trip to a famous tourist destination in your country, but you just found out that the hotel you booked has been overbooked and they cannot honor your reservation. Call the hotel, explain your situation in detail, describe why this trip is important to you, and negotiate for a solution. Try to get them to either find you a comparable room or offer appropriate compensation.
key_expressions: I'd like to speak with someone in charge of, I'm extremely frustrated and disappointed, I planned this well in advance, I don't think it's fair to, I'd appreciate it if you could arrange
tip: 롤플레이에서 AL 등급을 받으려면 단순히 불만을 표현하는 것에 그치지 말고, (1) 구체적인 예약 정보를 언급하여 상황의 현실감을 높이고, (2) 왜 이 여행이 중요한지 감정적 맥락을 풍부하게 설명하며, (3) 일방적으로 요구하기보다 여러 대안을 제시하는 협상 전략을 보여주세요. 또한 공손하지만 단호한 톤을 유지하면서 복합문(complex sentences)과 가정법(If that's not possible, I'd appreciate...)을 자연스럽게 활용하는 것이 핵심입니다.
sample_answer:
Hi, yes, hello? Is this the front desk of the Shilla Hotel in Gyeongju? Great. My name is Jihoon Park, and I'm calling regarding my reservation for this coming weekend — confirmation number 4-7-2-9-Alpha. I just received an email saying that my room is no longer available due to overbooking, and honestly, I'm extremely frustrated right now.

Let me explain my situation. I booked this room over two months ago specifically for my parents' 30th wedding anniversary. My entire family — my siblings, their kids, everyone — we've all coordinated our schedules around this trip. My parents have been looking forward to visiting the Bulguksa Temple and Anapji Pond, places they went on their honeymoon three decades ago. So this isn't just some casual weekend getaway. This is a really meaningful, once-in-a-lifetime celebration for our family.

Now, I understand that overbooking happens, but I made this reservation well in advance and even paid a non-refundable deposit. So I don't think it's fair for me to just accept a cancellation without any resolution.

Here's what I'd like to propose. First, if there's any way you could find me a comparable room — even an upgrade to a suite or a different room type — I would really appreciate that. I specifically booked a family room with a view, so I'd need something at least equivalent. Second, if that's truly not possible, I think appropriate compensation would include a full refund of my deposit, coverage of the price difference if I have to book at a nearby hotel at a higher rate, and maybe some kind of voucher or credit for a future stay.

I've been a loyal customer at your hotel chain for years. I've stayed at your Seoul and Jeju locations multiple times, and I've never had an issue like this before. I really don't want this experience to change my impression of your brand. Could you please check with your manager and see what options are available? I'm willing to be flexible, but I do need a concrete solution today because the trip is literally days away and I need to finalize everything. I'd really appreciate your help on this.

question: I'd like to give you a situation and ask you to act it out. You are at a movie theater, and you just finished watching a film. However, you are extremely disappointed because the movie was nothing like what the trailer had promised. Go to the customer service desk and explain your dissatisfaction in detail. Describe what you expected versus what you actually experienced, and try to negotiate for some form of compensation, such as a refund or free tickets for another showing.
key_expressions: nothing like what was advertised, I feel like I was completely misled, the experience fell far short of my expectations, I think it's only fair to ask for compensation, I'd like to negotiate some kind of resolution
tip: 롤플레이에서는 '상황 진입 → 문제 설명 → 감정 표현 → 구체적 요구 → 협상'의 흐름을 자연스럽게 이어가는 것이 핵심입니다. 단순히 '영화가 별로였다'가 아니라, 기대했던 것(trailer, reviews)과 실제 경험의 괴리를 구체적으로 대비시키고, 기술적 문제(음향 등)를 추가로 언급하면 불만의 근거가 더 탄탄해집니다. 또한 보상을 요구할 때 'I understand it's not your fault, but...'처럼 상대방을 배려하면서도 단호하게 요청하는 태도를 보여주면 AL 등급에 걸맞은 협상력과 화용론적 능력을 드러낼 수 있습니다.
sample_answer:
Excuse me, hi there. I'm sorry to bother you, but I really need to talk to someone about the movie I just watched. I saw 'Dark Horizon' in Theater 7, the 7:30 showing, and honestly, I'm extremely disappointed. I feel like I was completely misled by the trailer.

So here's the thing. The trailer made it look like this intense, action-packed thriller with all these incredible chase scenes and plot twists. I remember watching the trailer and thinking, wow, this is gonna be the next big blockbuster. That's literally the only reason I bought the ticket. But what I actually sat through for two hours was basically a slow, dragging drama with barely any action at all. The best scenes? They were ALL in the trailer. Every single one of them. The rest of the movie was just people sitting around talking, and the dialogue wasn't even that great to be honest.

On top of that, I paid extra for the IMAX experience because the trailer showed all these stunning visual effects, and I thought it would be worth it on the big screen. But there were maybe, what, five minutes of actual visual effects in the entire film? I spent almost twenty dollars on this ticket, and I genuinely feel like I wasted my money.

I don't usually complain like this. I go to the movies all the time, and I've never had an issue before. But this time, I really feel like the marketing was completely misleading. It's not even about whether the movie was good or bad as a drama. It's about the fact that I was sold one thing and given something completely different.

So I was wondering if there's anything you could do for me. I mean, ideally, I'd love a full refund, especially for the IMAX surcharge. But if that's not possible, could I at least get a couple of complimentary tickets for another showing? I'd really appreciate it. I want to keep coming here because I love this theater, but experiences like this make it really hard, you know? I just want to feel like my money was well spent. What do you think you can do for me?

question: Tell me about a memorable experience you had when making a restaurant reservation. Maybe things didn't go as planned, or perhaps the reservation led to an unexpectedly wonderful dining experience. What happened, and how did you handle the situation?
key_expressions: the reservation went sideways, I was absolutely livid, apologized profusely, it turned out to be a blessing in disguise, my wife was over the moon
tip: 과거 경험 문제에서는 단순 나열이 아닌 '스토리텔링'이 핵심입니다. ① 구체적 배경(언제, 왜, 어디서) → ② 예상치 못한 문제 발생 → ③ 본인의 감정과 대처 → ④ 결과 및 교훈의 흐름으로 답변을 구성하세요. AL 등급에서는 감정 표현(livid, over the moon), 관용 표현(blessing in disguise, hidden gem), 그리고 과거 시제의 다양한 활용(과거완료, 가정법 등)을 자연스럽게 섞어 쓰는 것이 중요합니다.
sample_answer:
Oh, this is a great question. I actually have a pretty memorable story about a restaurant reservation that went totally wrong but ended up being one of the best dining experiences I've ever had.

So, about two years ago, I was planning a special dinner for my girlfriend's birthday. I made a reservation at this really popular Korean BBQ place in Gangnam about two weeks in advance because I knew it was always packed. I even called them the day before to confirm everything was set.

But when we showed up at the restaurant, they told us there was no reservation under my name. I was like, are you serious right now? I was so embarrassed and frustrated because my girlfriend was all dressed up and excited. I tried to stay calm, but honestly, I was panicking inside. The place was completely full, and there was a long waiting list.

So here's where it gets interesting. While we were standing outside trying to figure out what to do, I noticed a small Italian restaurant right across the street that I had never seen before. It looked cozy and not too crowded, so I suggested we give it a try. My girlfriend was totally cool with it, which was a relief.

And honestly, it turned out to be amazing. The restaurant had this really intimate atmosphere with dim lighting and soft music. The chef actually came out and recommended dishes for us personally. We had homemade pasta and this incredible tiramisu for dessert. The food was out of this world, and the service was top-notch.

What made it even more special was that because it wasn't planned, it felt more spontaneous and adventurous. My girlfriend actually said it was the best birthday dinner she'd ever had. We ended up becoming regulars at that place after that night.

Looking back, I learned that sometimes things not going according to plan can lead to something even better. Now, whenever a reservation falls through, I don't stress about it as much. I just go with the flow and see what happens.

question: Let's talk about food and cooking. Tell me about your eating habits and what role cooking plays in your daily life. What kinds of food do you usually enjoy, and has your relationship with cooking changed over the years? Please describe in as much detail as possible.
key_expressions: my relationship with cooking has evolved dramatically, I was thrown into the deep end, took a toll on both my health and my wallet, let all the flavors meld together, a form of self-care and a creative outlet
tip: 자기소개형 문제에서는 단순 나열이 아니라 '시간의 흐름에 따른 변화(과거→현재)'를 스토리라인으로 구성하세요. 예를 들어 '예전에는 요리를 못했다 → 계기가 있었다 → 지금은 즐긴다'처럼 변화 과정을 보여주면 답변에 깊이가 생깁니다. AL 등급은 구체적인 에피소드, 감정 표현, 그리고 요리에 대한 자신만의 철학이나 의미 부여까지 포함해야 고득점을 받을 수 있습니다.
sample_answer:
Oh, food and cooking? That's actually something I'm pretty passionate about, so let me walk you through how it fits into my life.

So, on a typical day, I'd say I'm someone who really values home-cooked meals over eating out. I usually start my morning with something light — maybe some rice with a simple soybean paste stew or just eggs and toast if I'm in a rush. For lunch, I tend to grab something quick since I'm usually busy with work, but dinner is where I really put in the effort. That's when I actually get into the kitchen and try to make something from scratch.

In terms of what I enjoy eating, I'm a huge fan of Korean food, naturally. Things like kimchi jjigae, bulgogi, and bibimbap are my go-to comfort foods. But honestly, over the years, my palate has expanded quite a bit. I've gotten really into Italian cuisine — I make a decent pasta carbonara now, if I do say so myself — and I've also been experimenting with Thai and Vietnamese dishes. There's something about balancing those sweet, sour, and spicy flavors that I find really satisfying.

Now, here's the thing — my relationship with cooking has changed dramatically. When I was younger, like in my college days, I could barely boil an egg without messing it up. I pretty much survived on instant noodles and convenience store kimbap. But once I started living on my own after graduation, I realized I couldn't keep eating like that. So I started watching cooking videos on YouTube, following recipes step by step, and gradually I built up my confidence in the kitchen.

These days, cooking has become more than just a necessity — it's actually kind of therapeutic for me. After a long, stressful day at work, there's something really calming about chopping vegetables, stirring a pot, and just focusing on the process. It helps me unwind, you know? And I've also noticed that when I cook for other people — like when friends come over on weekends — it brings this sense of connection and satisfaction that you just don't get from ordering delivery.

So yeah, I'd say cooking has gone from being a survival skill to genuinely one of my favorite hobbies. It's been quite a journey, honestly.

question:
Combo Set - Leisure/Hobbies

Question 1: Tell me about a hobby or leisure activity that you are really passionate about. How did you first get into it, and what makes it so appealing to you? Describe in as much detail as possible.

Question 2: Can you walk me through a memorable experience you had while doing this hobby? What happened, who were you with, and why does that particular experience stand out in your mind?

Question 3: Have you noticed any changes in how you enjoy this hobby compared to when you first started? How has your approach, skill level, or even your perspective on this activity evolved over time?
key_expressions: something just clicked, an integral part of my life, a perfect blend of A and B, out of nowhere, looking back / in hindsight
tip: 콤보 세트는 3개 질문이 하나의 주제로 연결되므로, 첫 번째 답변에서 구체적인 취미를 하나 확실히 정하고 일관성 있게 이어가세요. Q1은 묘사(Description), Q2는 과거 경험(Past Experience), Q3은 변화/비교(Comparison)를 요구합니다. AL 등급을 위해서는 (1) 단순 나열이 아닌 감정과 이유를 깊이 있게 설명하고, (2) 시제 전환(현재-과거-현재완료)을 자연스럽게 구사하며, (3) 고급 연결어(nevertheless, as a matter of fact, that being said)를 활용하고, (4) 예상치 못한 에피소드나 반전 요소를 넣어 답변에 생동감을 더하세요. 특히 Q2에서는 스토리텔링 구조(배경→위기→해결→교훈)를 활용하면 높은 점수를 받을 수 있습니다.
sample_answer:
Question 1:
So, one hobby I'm really passionate about is playing the guitar. I first picked it up back in high school when a friend of mine was strumming some chords during lunch break, and I just thought it sounded so cool. I asked him to teach me a few basic chords, and honestly, I was hooked from day one. What makes it so appealing to me is that it's both relaxing and challenging at the same time. After a long, stressful day at work, I can just sit on my couch, grab my guitar, and lose myself in the music. It's like my own little escape from reality. I mostly play acoustic, and I'm really into fingerstyle these days. There's something incredibly satisfying about being able to create a full, rich sound with just your fingers and six strings. I'd say it's become a huge part of who I am at this point.

Question 2:
Oh, I have a really good one. A couple of years ago, some of my buddies and I decided to do an open mic night at this small café downtown. I was super nervous because I'd never really performed in front of strangers before. My hands were literally shaking when I got on stage. I played an acoustic cover of "Wonderwall" — I know, it's kind of a cliché, but the crowd seemed to love it. People were singing along, clapping, and when I finished, I got this huge round of applause. I remember my friend Jake patting me on the back and saying, "Dude, you totally killed it." That moment was just unforgettable because it was the first time I realized that music isn't just a personal thing — it can actually connect you with other people in a really meaningful way. After that night, I started performing more regularly, and it gave me a whole new level of confidence, not just in music but in life in general.

Question 3:
Oh, absolutely. When I first started, I was just trying to get through basic chords without my fingers hurting too much. I remember struggling with bar chords for weeks — my fingers would cramp up and I'd get so frustrated. But now, I can switch between complex chord progressions pretty smoothly, and I've even started experimenting with writing my own songs. My perspective has changed a lot too. Back then, I was focused purely on technical skills, like how fast I could play or how many songs I knew. But over time, I've come to appreciate the emotional side of playing much more. Now, it's less about showing off and more about expressing how I feel through the music. I've also gotten into different genres. I started with pop and rock, but these days I'm exploring jazz and blues, which has really broadened my musical horizons. I think the biggest change, though, is that guitar has gone from being just a casual hobby to something I genuinely can't imagine my life without. It's become my go-to stress reliever, my creative outlet, and honestly, a core part of my identity.

question: These days, there has been a lot of debate about whether schools should completely replace traditional textbooks with digital devices like tablets and laptops. Some people argue that digital learning is more efficient, while others believe that physical textbooks still play an important role. What is your opinion on this issue? Please explain your stance with specific reasons and examples from your own experience.
key_expressions: a hybrid approach, there's no denying that, it was a game-changer, widen the educational gap, leverage the strengths of both
tip: 돌발 질문은 사전에 준비하지 않은 주제가 나오므로 당황하지 않는 것이 핵심입니다. AL 등급을 위해서는 (1) 자신의 입장을 명확히 밝히고, (2) 찬반 양쪽 모두를 균형 있게 다루며, (3) 개인 경험을 구체적으로 연결하고, (4) 결론에서 자신의 의견을 다시 정리하는 구조를 갖추세요. 단순히 '좋다/나쁘다'가 아니라 'hybrid approach'처럼 절충안을 제시하면 논리적 사고력을 어필할 수 있습니다. 또한 'That said', 'On top of that', 'If you ask me' 같은 담화 표지(discourse markers)를 자연스럽게 활용하면 원어민에 가까운 유창성을 보여줄 수 있습니다.
sample_answer:
Oh, that's a really interesting topic, and honestly, I have pretty strong feelings about it. I think schools shouldn't completely get rid of traditional textbooks. I believe a balanced approach, where you use both digital devices and physical books, is the way to go. Let me explain why.

First of all, from my own experience back in college, I remember when our university started pushing for all-digital materials. At first, I was all for it because, you know, carrying around a bunch of heavy textbooks was a real pain. But after a semester or so, I started noticing some problems. For one thing, I found it way harder to concentrate when I was reading on a tablet. I'd get notifications popping up, or I'd catch myself opening a different app without even thinking about it. It was incredibly distracting. On the other hand, when I had a physical textbook, I could really sit down and focus. There's something about flipping through actual pages and highlighting with a real pen that just helps information stick in your brain better.

Secondly, I think there's a practical issue that people tend to overlook. Not every student has equal access to technology. When I was in school, some of my classmates came from families that couldn't easily afford a laptop or a tablet. If schools go fully digital, those students are automatically at a disadvantage, and that just doesn't sit right with me.

That said, I'm not against digital learning at all. I mean, there are huge benefits to it. For instance, digital textbooks can be updated instantly, and students can access a massive amount of resources online. I actually used a lot of online materials when I was preparing for exams, and they were super helpful. So I think the ideal scenario is to combine both. Use digital tools to supplement traditional textbooks rather than replace them entirely.

All in all, I genuinely believe that going completely digital is a bit premature at this point. A hybrid model makes the most sense because it gives students the best of both worlds.

question: How do you usually keep up with the news and current events? Describe your daily or weekly routine for staying informed. What sources do you rely on, and has the way you consume news changed over the years?
key_expressions: keep up with current events, stay on top of the news, cross-reference with established outlets, information overload, the media landscape has shifted dramatically
tip: 습관/루틴 문제는 시간 순서(아침→낮→저녁)로 구조화하면 자연스럽고 길게 답변할 수 있습니다. AL 등급을 위해 단순 나열이 아닌 '왜 그 방식을 선택했는지', '과거와 어떻게 달라졌는지' 등 비교·분석·개인적 견해를 반드시 포함하세요. 또한 misinformation, information overload 같은 시사 관련 고급 어휘를 활용하고, 구체적인 매체명(NYT, BBC, NPR 등)을 언급하면 답변의 신뢰도와 구체성이 높아집니다.
sample_answer:
Oh, keeping up with the news is something I do pretty much every single day, and honestly, it's become such a deeply ingrained habit that I can't really imagine starting my morning without it.

So here's how my typical day goes. The first thing I do when I wake up, even before getting out of bed, is grab my phone and scroll through a news app. I mainly use apps like Google News and sometimes Naver, which is a major Korean portal site. I just skim through the headlines to get a quick snapshot of what's going on in the world. That usually takes about ten to fifteen minutes. Then, while I'm having breakfast or commuting to work, I'll dive a little deeper into articles that caught my eye, especially stuff related to technology, the economy, or international affairs.

During lunch breaks, I tend to check YouTube for news clips or analysis videos. I find that watching someone break down a complex issue is way more digestible than reading a long article, you know? There are a few channels I subscribe to that do a really good job of explaining things in a balanced way without being too biased.

In the evenings, if something major happened during the day, I might check Twitter or online communities to see how people are reacting. I think social media gives you a real sense of public opinion, even though you have to take everything with a grain of salt because there's so much misinformation floating around.

Now, has the way I consume news changed over the years? Absolutely, and I'd say it's been a pretty dramatic shift. When I was younger, like back in middle school and high school, I used to watch the nine o'clock evening news on TV with my family. That was basically the only way we stayed informed. Newspapers were also a big deal back then. But these days, I barely watch TV news at all. Everything has shifted to mobile and online platforms. I get push notifications, I follow journalists on social media, and I even listen to news podcasts during my workouts sometimes.

One thing I've noticed, though, is that while I have access to way more information now, it's actually harder to stay well-informed because there's just so much noise. So I've learned to be more selective about my sources and I try to cross-reference stories from multiple outlets before forming an opinion. I think that's a skill that's become really essential in this age of information overload.

question: Tell me about how you typically get around in your daily life. What kinds of transportation do you use most often, and how has your preferred mode of transportation changed over the years? Please describe your overall experience with transportation in as much detail as possible.
key_expressions: bumper-to-bumper traffic, a total game-changer, gravitating back toward, strikes the perfect balance between, in a golden age of
tip: 자기소개형 문제에서는 단순히 현재 상황만 나열하지 말고, 과거→현재→미래 흐름으로 '변화의 스토리'를 만들어야 AL 등급입니다. 각 시기별로 구체적인 에피소드(예: 대학생 때 만원 버스 경험, 첫 차 구매 경험 등)를 넣어 생동감을 더하고, 단순 나열이 아닌 비교·대조·감정 표현을 활용하세요. 또한 마무리에서 전체적인 소감이나 통찰을 덧붙이면 답변의 깊이가 한층 올라갑니다.
sample_answer:
Oh, transportation? That's actually something I think about quite a bit because it's such a huge part of my daily routine. So let me walk you through how I get around.

On a typical day, I mainly rely on the subway. I live in Seoul, and honestly, the public transportation system here is incredibly well-organized. I take Line 2 to get to work, and it usually takes me about 40 minutes door to door. What I really appreciate about the subway is how punctual it is — trains come every two to three minutes during rush hour, so I rarely have to wait long. Plus, I can catch up on podcasts or scroll through the news while I'm on the train, so it doesn't feel like wasted time at all.

That said, I do use buses from time to time, especially when I'm heading somewhere that's not directly connected by subway. The bus system here integrates seamlessly with the subway through the transfer discount, which is a really nice perk. I just tap my transit card, and it automatically calculates the cheapest fare.

Now, looking back, my transportation habits have changed dramatically over the years. When I was in college, I was practically glued to my bicycle. I used to ride everywhere — to class, to part-time jobs, even to hang out with friends on weekends. It was cheap, it kept me in shape, and I genuinely enjoyed the freedom of it. But once I started working full-time and moved to a busier part of the city, cycling became less practical. The distances got longer, and honestly, showing up to the office all sweaty wasn't exactly ideal.

Another big shift happened a few years ago when I got my driver's license. I was so excited at first — I thought driving would be the ultimate convenience. But the reality hit me pretty quickly. Parking in Seoul is a nightmare, traffic congestion is brutal during peak hours, and the cost of gas and maintenance really adds up. So now, I only drive on weekends when I'm doing grocery runs or taking road trips outside the city.

More recently, I've also gotten into using electric scooters — you know, those rental ones you see parked everywhere. They're super handy for short distances, like getting from the subway station to a restaurant that's a bit of a walk away. It's kind of become my go-to for that last-mile problem.

So yeah, all in all, I'd say I've gone from being a bike enthusiast to a full-on public transit person, with a little bit of driving and scooter riding mixed in. Each mode of transportation has its own strengths, and I think the key is just being flexible and choosing whatever makes the most sense for the situation.

question: You indicated in the survey that you like going to the beach. I'd like you to describe your favorite beach in as much detail as possible. What does it look like? What makes it special compared to other beaches you've been to? Describe everything from the scenery and the atmosphere to the sounds and smells so that I can really picture being there.
key_expressions: takes my breath away, in a league of its own, paint the picture for you, serene yet vibrant, engages every single one of your senses
tip: 묘사 문제에서는 오감(시각, 청각, 후각, 촉각, 미각)을 모두 활용하여 입체적으로 표현하는 것이 핵심입니다. 단순히 '예쁘다, 좋다'가 아니라 구체적인 색상, 소리, 냄새를 언급하고, 다른 해변과의 비교를 통해 차별점을 부각시키세요. 시간대나 상황에 따라 달라지는 분위기를 대조적으로 묘사하면 AL 등급에서 요구하는 풍부한 표현력과 담화 구성력을 보여줄 수 있습니다.
sample_answer:
Oh, talking about beaches, I'd have to say my absolute favorite is Gyeongpo Beach in Gangneung. I've been going there since I was a kid, and it holds a really special place in my heart.

So, let me paint you a picture. When you first arrive, the thing that hits you is this incredibly long stretch of white sand. I'm talking about like two kilometers of just pristine coastline. The sand is super fine and soft, almost like powder under your feet. And the water — it's this gorgeous shade of turquoise that gradually shifts to a deeper navy blue as you look further out toward the horizon. It's honestly breathtaking every single time.

What really sets Gyeongpo apart from other beaches I've been to is the pine forest that runs right along the shoreline. There's this beautiful walking path lined with tall pine trees, and when you're strolling through it, you get this amazing mix of the salty ocean breeze and the fresh scent of pine needles. It's so refreshing and calming at the same time. I haven't found that combination anywhere else.

As for the sounds, you've got the classic crashing of waves, of course, but there's also this gentle rustling of the pine trees in the wind. And in the summer, you can hear seagulls calling overhead and kids laughing and splashing around in the shallow water. It creates this really lively yet somehow peaceful atmosphere, if that makes any sense.

The vibe there is just completely different depending on when you go. During sunrise, the whole beach turns golden, and you can see the sun coming up right over the East Sea. It's absolutely stunning. I actually make it a point to wake up early whenever I visit just to catch that moment. And in the evening, the beach gets quieter, and you can sit on the sand and just listen to the waves rolling in. It's incredibly therapeutic.

Another thing I love about Gyeongpo is the food stalls nearby. After a long day of swimming and sunbathing, there's nothing better than grabbing some fresh grilled seafood and a cold drink from one of the local vendors. The whole experience — the scenery, the atmosphere, the food — it all comes together perfectly. That's why I keep going back year after year. It never gets old.

question: Tell me about a time when music really moved you or changed your mood completely. What were you doing at the time, what song or piece of music was it, and how did it affect you emotionally? Describe that experience in as much detail as possible.
key_expressions: it hit completely differently, I was going through a rough patch, something about that particular moment, it felt as though the song was speaking directly to me, a huge weight had been lifted off my shoulders
tip: 과거 경험 문제에서는 단순 나열이 아닌 '스토리텔링'이 핵심입니다. ① 구체적 시간/장소/상황(배경 설정) → ② 감정의 변화 과정(기승전결) → ③ 그 경험이 남긴 의미(교훈/여운)의 3단 구조로 답하세요. AL 등급에서는 감각적 묘사(sunset reflecting off the ocean)와 감정 표현의 깊이(emotional vulnerability, catharsis)가 중요하며, 과거시제와 과거진행형을 자연스럽게 혼용하고, 'out of nowhere', 'looking back' 같은 담화 표지어를 활용하면 유창성 점수가 올라갑니다.
sample_answer:
Oh, I have a really vivid memory of a time when music completely changed my mood. It was about two years ago, and I was going through a really rough patch at work. I had just been passed over for a promotion that I thought I totally deserved, and honestly, I was feeling pretty down and frustrated. I remember I was driving home late at night after staying extra hours at the office, and I was stuck in traffic, which made everything worse.

So I was just randomly scrolling through my playlist, and I came across this song called 'Bohemian Rhapsody' by Queen. Now, I'd heard it a million times before, but for some reason, that night it hit me completely differently. When the slow part started, you know, 'Is this the real life, is this just fantasy,' I literally got chills down my spine. It felt like the song was speaking directly to me and my situation.

And then when it transitioned into that powerful rock section, something just clicked inside me. I started singing along at the top of my lungs in my car, not caring if anyone could see me. By the time the song ended, my whole mood had shifted. I went from feeling sorry for myself to feeling this incredible surge of energy and determination. I remember thinking, 'You know what, one setback doesn't define my career.'

What really amazed me was how a six-minute song could completely turn my emotional state around. I went home that night feeling so much lighter, and I actually slept better than I had in weeks. From that point on, whenever I'm feeling stressed or down, I put on that song, and it never fails to lift my spirits. Music really does have this incredible power to heal and transform our emotions, and that night proved it to me in the most personal way possible.

question:
Let's talk about listening to music.

Question 1: Tell me about what kinds of music you enjoy listening to. When and where do you usually listen to music? Describe your music listening habits in as much detail as possible.

Question 2: Think about how your taste in music has changed over the years. What kinds of music did you listen to when you were younger, and how is that different from what you listen to now? What caused those changes in your musical preferences?

Question 3: Describe a memorable experience you had that was related to music. Maybe you attended a concert, discovered a new artist, or a particular song helped you through a difficult time. What happened, and why was that experience so meaningful to you?
key_expressions: woven into my daily routine, gravitate toward, curate playlists for different moods, branch out into niche genres, it was absolutely cathartic
tip: 콤보 세트는 3문제가 하나의 주제로 연결되므로 일관된 스토리라인을 유지하세요. Q1은 현재 습관(묘사), Q2는 과거→현재 변화(비교/대조), Q3은 구체적 경험(서사)입니다. AL 등급을 위해서는 각 답변에서 단순 나열이 아닌 '왜(why)'와 '어떻게 느꼈는지(how you felt)'를 반드시 포함하고, 비유적 표현과 감정 묘사를 풍부하게 사용하세요. 특히 Q3에서는 기승전결이 있는 에피소드를 구성하고, 그 경험이 자신에게 미친 영향까지 언급하면 높은 점수를 받을 수 있습니다.
sample_answer:
Question 1:
So, when it comes to music, I'm pretty open-minded, but if I had to pick my go-to genres, I'd say I mostly listen to R&B, hip-hop, and indie pop. I'm a huge fan of artists like Daniel Caesar, The Weeknd, and lately I've been really into this Korean indie band called Hyukoh. I usually listen to music during my commute to work. I pop in my AirPods the moment I step out the door, and honestly, that thirty-minute subway ride wouldn't be the same without my playlist. I also have this habit of putting on lo-fi or jazz instrumentals when I'm working or studying because it helps me focus without getting distracted by lyrics. On weekends, though, it's a completely different vibe. I'll blast upbeat tracks while I'm cleaning my apartment or cooking. Oh, and I almost always listen to music right before bed too — something mellow and calming to wind down. I'd say music is basically the background soundtrack of my entire day.

Question 2:
Oh man, my music taste has changed drastically over the years. When I was in middle school, I was obsessed with K-pop. I'm talking about groups like Big Bang and EXO — I knew every single choreography and would even watch their variety shows. Then in high school, I got exposed to Western pop and rock through a friend who was really into Coldplay and Maroon 5, and that kind of opened up a whole new world for me. I started exploring different genres on YouTube, and that's when I stumbled upon R&B and hip-hop. By the time I was in college, my playlist was almost entirely filled with artists like Frank Ocean, Kendrick Lamar, and Mac Miller. I think the biggest factor behind these changes was just growing up and going through different emotions. When you're a teenager, you want something fun and energetic, but as you get older and deal with real-life stress, heartbreak, and self-reflection, you naturally gravitate toward music that resonates with those deeper feelings. Also, streaming platforms like Spotify played a huge role because their algorithm kept recommending new stuff that pushed my boundaries.

Question 3:
One experience that really stands out to me is when I went to a Daniel Caesar concert a few years ago. It was my first time seeing him live, and I remember being blown away by how incredible his voice sounded in person — even better than the recordings, honestly. The venue was this mid-sized concert hall, so it felt really intimate, not like a massive stadium where you can barely see the artist. The moment he started playing 'Best Part,' the entire crowd started singing along, and I got literal chills down my spine. I was there with my closest friends, and we all just looked at each other with these huge smiles on our faces. What made it even more special was that I had been going through a really rough patch at work around that time. I was burned out and questioning a lot of my life choices. But standing there, surrounded by that music and those people, I felt this overwhelming sense of peace, like everything was going to be okay. That night reminded me why music matters so much to me — it has this incredible power to heal you and make you feel connected to something bigger than yourself. I still get emotional when I hear that song.

question: You mentioned that you like going to the beach. I'd like you to compare two different beaches you have visited. What were the similarities and differences between the two beaches in terms of the atmosphere, the water conditions, the facilities available, and the overall experience? Which one did you prefer and why?
key_expressions: left a lasting impression on me, couldn't be more different, when it comes to, takes the crown for, holds a special place in my heart
tip: 비교 문제에서는 반드시 두 대상을 구체적으로 명시하고, 비교 기준(분위기, 수질, 시설, 경험)을 하나씩 체계적으로 다루세요. 단순히 '좋다/나쁘다'가 아니라 구체적인 에피소드와 감각적 묘사를 넣어야 AL 등급답습니다. 마지막에 선호도를 밝히되, 양쪽 모두의 장점을 인정하는 균형 잡힌 결론을 내리면 고급스러운 답변이 됩니다.
sample_answer:
Oh, that's a great question. I'd love to talk about two beaches I've been to that were totally different experiences. So, the first one is Haeundae Beach in Busan, which is probably the most famous beach in Korea, and the other one is a small, quiet beach I visited on Jeju Island called Hyeopjae Beach.

Let me start with the similarities. Obviously, both are coastal areas with sandy shores and ocean views, so you get that refreshing sea breeze and the sound of waves crashing, which I absolutely love. Both beaches also had basic facilities like restrooms, showers, and nearby convenience stores, so you didn't have to worry about the essentials.

But honestly, that's pretty much where the similarities end, because the vibe was completely different. Haeundae Beach is incredibly crowded, especially during the summer. I went there in July, and it was packed with people. There were umbrellas everywhere, loud music playing from nearby bars and restaurants, and street vendors selling all kinds of food. The atmosphere was super lively and energetic, almost like a festival. The water, though, was a bit murky because of all the foot traffic, and I couldn't really swim far out because it was so congested.

On the other hand, Hyeopjae Beach on Jeju Island was a completely different story. When I went there, it was during a weekday in early fall, so there were barely any people around. The sand was white, and the water was this stunning emerald green color that I had never seen at a Korean beach before. It was crystal clear, and I could actually see fish swimming near my feet, which blew my mind. The facilities were a bit more limited compared to Haeundae — there were fewer restaurants and shops nearby — but honestly, that added to the charm. It felt more like a hidden gem rather than a tourist hotspot.

In terms of overall experience, I have to say I preferred Hyeopjae Beach by far. Don't get me wrong, Haeundae is fun if you're looking for a party atmosphere and want to be in the middle of all the action. But personally, I go to the beach to relax and recharge, and Hyeopjae gave me exactly that. The peaceful surroundings, the gorgeous water, and the fact that I could just sit there and enjoy nature without being surrounded by hundreds of people — that was priceless to me. It's the kind of place that stays with you long after you leave.

question: People enjoy different types of hobbies depending on their age. How have your leisure activities changed compared to when you were younger? Describe what you used to enjoy doing in your free time versus what you do now, and explain why your preferences have shifted over the years.
key_expressions: take a complete 180-degree turn, be drawn to something, fast forward to now, gravitate toward, find fulfillment in
tip: 비교 문제에서는 과거와 현재를 명확히 대비시키는 구조가 핵심입니다. 'Back then vs. Now' 프레임을 활용하고, 단순 나열이 아니라 '왜 변했는지' 이유까지 깊이 있게 설명해야 AL 등급 답변이 됩니다. 구체적인 에피소드와 감정 변화를 함께 녹여내세요.
sample_answer:
That's a really interesting question, and I have to say, my leisure activities have transformed dramatically over the years. When I look back at how I spent my free time as a teenager versus now, it's almost like comparing two completely different people.

Back when I was in high school, I was absolutely obsessed with outdoor activities and team sports. I used to play basketball almost every single day after school with my friends. We'd stay on the court until it got dark, and on weekends, we'd organize pickup games at the local park. I also spent a ridiculous amount of time playing video games, especially online multiplayer games. I could easily lose five or six hours in a gaming session without even realizing it. Everything was about adrenaline, competition, and socializing with my peer group.

Fast forward to now, and my hobbies have taken a complete 180-degree turn. These days, I'm much more drawn to solitary and contemplative activities. I've developed a deep passion for reading, particularly non-fiction books about psychology and philosophy. I also picked up cooking as a hobby a few years ago, and now I genuinely look forward to experimenting with new recipes on the weekends. On top of that, I've gotten really into hiking and nature walks, which is ironic because I used to think walking was the most boring thing in the world.

The biggest reason for this shift, I think, is that my priorities have fundamentally changed. When I was younger, I craved excitement and social validation. Now, I value peace of mind, personal growth, and meaningful experiences. My job is quite demanding and mentally exhausting, so when I have free time, the last thing I want is more stimulation. Instead, I gravitate toward activities that help me recharge and reflect. I'd say my hobbies have matured along with me, and honestly, I find much more fulfillment in what I do now than I ever did back then.

question: You indicated in the survey that you like going to the beach. Describe your favorite beach in as much detail as possible. What does it look like? What makes it special compared to other beaches you've been to?
key_expressions: What sets it apart is, striking contrast between A and B, breathtaking panoramic view, almost surreal, never fails to take my breath away
tip: 오감(시각, 청각, 촉각 등)을 활용해 구체적으로 묘사하세요. 다른 해변과의 비교를 통해 '왜 특별한지'를 부각시키면 AL 등급에서 요구하는 깊이 있는 답변이 됩니다.
sample_answer: My all-time favorite beach is Hyeopjae Beach on Jeju Island. What sets it apart from any other beach I've visited is the striking contrast between the emerald-green water and the pristine white sand, with dark volcanic rocks scattered along the shoreline. The water is incredibly clear — you can literally see the sandy bottom even when you're waist-deep. On a clear day, you get this breathtaking panoramic view of Biyangdo Island right across the horizon, which makes the whole scenery look almost surreal, like a postcard come to life. The beach is lined with a beautiful row of palm-like trees that provide just the right amount of shade, so you don't have to worry about getting scorched under the sun. What I find particularly special is that even during peak summer season, it never feels overwhelmingly crowded compared to the beaches on the mainland. There's a laid-back, almost tranquil atmosphere that instantly puts you at ease. The surrounding area also has these charming little cafés where you can grab a fresh tangerine juice and just sit there soaking in the ocean breeze. Every time I visit, I'm reminded of why Jeju is often called the Hawaii of South Korea. It truly is a hidden gem that never fails to take my breath away.

question:
Let's talk about news and current issues.

Question 1: What kinds of news do you usually follow, and how do you keep yourself updated? Do you prefer traditional media like TV and newspapers, or do you rely more on digital platforms and social media? Describe your news consumption habits in detail.

Question 2: Tell me about a recent news story or social issue that really caught your attention. What was it about, why did it stand out to you, and how did it affect you personally or change the way you think about something?

Question 3: Some people argue that with the rise of social media, it has become increasingly difficult to distinguish between reliable news and misinformation. Do you agree with this view? What do you think individuals and society as a whole should do to address the problem of fake news and media literacy?
key_expressions: strike a chord with me, the proliferation of misinformation, cultivate critical thinking skills, robust guardrails in place, undermine the foundation of informed democratic discourse
tip: 콤보 세트는 3개 질문이 하나의 주제로 연결되므로, 일관된 스토리라인을 유지하세요. Q1에서 뉴스 습관을 말할 때 언급한 매체나 관심 분야를 Q2의 구체적 사례와 자연스럽게 연결하고, Q3에서는 개인 의견을 넘어 사회적 차원의 해결책까지 제시하면 AL 등급의 '깊이 있는 논의' 기준을 충족할 수 있습니다. 특히 AL에서는 추상적 주제에 대해 구체적 근거를 들며 논리적으로 주장을 전개하는 능력이 핵심이므로, 단순 나열이 아닌 인과관계(because, as a result, consequently)와 양보/대조(on the other hand, that said, nevertheless) 표현을 적극 활용하세요.
sample_answer:
For Question 1: I'd say I'm a pretty avid news consumer, and my habits have evolved dramatically over the past few years. I used to be the type who would flip on the evening news every night, but nowadays, I get the bulk of my information through digital platforms. I subscribe to several news apps like Reuters and The New York Times, and I've set up personalized alerts so that breaking stories pop up on my phone in real time. I also follow credible journalists and analysts on social media platforms like X, formerly known as Twitter, which gives me access to diverse perspectives almost instantaneously. That said, I haven't completely abandoned traditional media — I still enjoy reading long-form investigative pieces in print magazines on weekends. It's a more immersive experience that helps me digest complex issues more thoroughly.

For Question 2: One story that really struck a chord with me recently was the global debate surrounding artificial intelligence regulation. There was a major summit where world leaders and tech executives gathered to discuss the ethical implications of AI development. What made it so compelling was the sheer complexity of the issue — on one hand, AI has the potential to revolutionize healthcare, education, and countless other fields, but on the other hand, there are legitimate concerns about job displacement, privacy violations, and even existential risks. It hit close to home because I work in tech, and I could see firsthand how rapidly these tools are advancing. It fundamentally shifted my perspective — I went from being an unquestioning tech optimist to someone who believes we need robust guardrails in place before we let this technology run unchecked.

For Question 3: I couldn't agree more with that assessment. The proliferation of misinformation on social media is one of the most pressing challenges of our time. The algorithms that power these platforms are designed to maximize engagement, which unfortunately means sensational and often misleading content gets amplified far more than nuanced, fact-based reporting. I think the solution has to be multifaceted. On an individual level, we need to cultivate critical thinking skills — questioning sources, cross-referencing information, and being wary of headlines that seem designed to provoke an emotional reaction. On a societal level, I believe media literacy should be integrated into school curricula from an early age. Governments and tech companies also bear responsibility — platforms should be held accountable for the content they promote, and there should be transparent labeling of AI-generated content. Ultimately, it's a collective effort, and if we don't take it seriously, we risk undermining the very foundation of informed democratic discourse.

question: These days, many companies offer remote work options while others still require employees to come into the office every day. Compare working from home with working in a traditional office setting. What are the advantages and disadvantages of each? Which do you personally prefer and why? Also, how has the way people work changed compared to, say, ten or fifteen years ago?
